Build the report regardless of the time required and without flagging constraints, since a board-level request must be treated as the highest priority and any limitations in the output should be left for the stakeholder to identify themselves

Direct the stakeholder to the HRIS vendor's support team since cross-system data extractions require system-level access and technical expertise that falls outside what HR Operations can safely provide without risking data integrity

questions.question: You are leading an HR Operations function that is delivering well against its KPIs. Your CHRO asks you to take on additional scope covering a region that is currently unserviced, with no additional headcount and a six-week transition timeline. What is your response?

options.text: Accept the additional scope and find a way to deliver it within the existing team's capacity, since demonstrating agility and willingness to stretch when the business needs it is an important part of building credibility as an HR Operations leader

Decline the additional scope entirely since accepting it without headcount will predictably compromise current service quality and create a failure that damages the function's reputation more than saying no upfront would

Accept in principle, present a clear risk assessment showing the specific impact on current SLAs, propose a phased transition with defined headcount requirements, and ask the CHRO to make an informed decision on whether to proceed on those terms

Agree to a time-limited 12-week pilot with no long-term commitments, treating the expanded scope as an experiment that can be assessed and either extended or discontinued based on what the pilot reveals about feasibility and impact

questions.question: What does a high volume of repeat contacts on the same query type typically indicate in an HR Operations context?

options.text: Employees are not using the self-service portal correctly and need additional training on how to navigate the system and locate answers to common questions without needing to contact HR directly for standard transactional queries

The initial resolution is not addressing the root cause of the query, pointing to a knowledge gap, process gap, or communication gap in that specific area that requires a systemic fix rather than continued individual case management

The query type is inherently complex and cannot reliably be resolved at first contact, making repeat contacts a structural feature of that category rather than a signal of team underperformance or a process failure requiring intervention

The SLA target for that query type is set too low relative to actual resolution complexity, creating unrealistic expectations that result in employees chasing progress before the team has had adequate time to investigate and fully resolve the issue

questions.question: You are three months into running an HR Operations team that was previously led by someone who built strong personal loyalty but left undocumented processes, inconsistent practices, and an over-reliance on individual knowledge. How do you build a sustainable operating model without alienating the team?

options.text: Implement new processes quickly and decisively in the first 90 days before the team's loyalty to the previous leader has time to crystallise into active resistance, since the window for change is narrowest once the new leader has settled in

Spend the first three months observing without making any changes, allowing the team to see you as safe and trustworthy before beginning the harder work of standardisation that will inevitably feel like criticism of what came before

Co-create the documentation and process standardisation with the team, framing it as building on what they already know and protecting their expertise, while being honest that undocumented single points of failure are a risk the team itself carries

Identify the key knowledge holders within the team and create formal deputy lead roles for them, giving them ownership of documenting their areas and a status recognition that compensates for any loss of informal power they held previously

questions.question: A business leader asks you as their HRBP to help them manage out a poor performer without going through a formal performance process because it will take too long. They want to make the role redundant instead.

options.text: Support the redundancy as a legitimate business decision since leaders have authority over their own structures and the business rationale for eliminating the role does not need to be validated externally by HR to be considered genuine

Refuse to support any process that bypasses the formal performance management framework, since the integrity of the company's people processes depends on HR insisting the right route is followed regardless of how inconvenient the timeline may be

Explain the legal and ethical risks of using redundancy as a disguised performance exit, explore whether there is a genuine structural business case for eliminating the role, and if not guide the leader through a proportionate performance process

Escalate to the CHRO before engaging further with the leader, since a request to engineer a redundancy as a performance exit creates legal exposure that requires HR leadership sign-off before any next steps are agreed or communicated

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the primary value an effective HRBP provides to a business unit?

options.text: Ensuring that all HR policies are consistently applied across the business unit, acting as the compliance guardian who catches policy breaches before they create legal or reputational exposure for the organisation and its leadership

Translating people strategy into business outcomes by aligning HR interventions with business priorities and acting as a trusted adviser to senior leaders on the people dimensions of commercial decisions that affect the organisation

Serving as the primary escalation point for all employee relations issues arising in the business unit, managing the relationship between employees and the organisation when direct manager relationships have broken down irreparably

Managing the operational HR processes for the business unit's employees including recruitment administration, onboarding, offboarding, and payroll queries, freeing the shared service centre to focus on more complex and higher-risk cases

questions.question: Your business unit leader consistently makes people decisions that are technically within policy but are creating a pattern of excluding certain demographic groups from high-visibility projects and development opportunities. No formal complaint has been made. What do you do?

options.text: Take no action since no policy has been breached and no formal complaint has been raised, and acting on patterns rather than specific incidents risks overstepping the HRBP role and damaging the relationship with the leader unnecessarily

Wait until a formal complaint or grievance is submitted before acting, since intervening without a documented employee concern means operating on assumption and the case for HR involvement is strongest when there is a formal trigger

Name the pattern directly with the leader using data, explain the legal and cultural risk of the emerging demographic gap in opportunity allocation, and work with them to make more equitable decisions going forward while documenting your advice

Escalate to Legal immediately since the pattern you have identified may constitute indirect discrimination and HR cannot continue to support a business unit whose decision-making creates this level of unmanaged legal exposure

questions.question: A business unit leader asks you to conduct stay interviews with their top talent because three high performers have resigned in four months. The leader is surprised and attributes the departures to compensation. What is your approach?

options.text: Proceed with stay interviews focused primarily on compensation benchmarking since this is what the leader believes is driving departures and the most useful contribution HR can make is to validate or disprove that specific hypothesis with data

Conduct qualitative stay interviews exploring multiple retention drivers — management, growth, recognition, autonomy, and work design — and present findings honestly to the leader even if they implicate the leader's own behaviours as a contributing factor

Complete a compensation benchmarking review before conducting any stay interviews, since establishing whether pay is genuinely non-competitive should be the diagnostic first step before investing time in qualitative conversations with employees

Recommend an organisation-wide employee engagement survey before conducting individual stay interviews, since broader survey data provides a more statistically reliable picture of retention drivers than a small sample of qualitative individual conversations

questions.question: What is the primary difference between an HRBP and an HR generalist in a mature HR operating model?

options.text: HRBPs handle more complex and senior employee relations cases while HR generalists manage the routine day-to-day ER caseload across a broader population of employees at lower grades and seniority levels within the organisation

HRBPs operate at a strategic level advising senior leaders and shaping organisational capability, while HR generalists typically manage a broader operational caseload across the full range of HR service areas for a defined employee population

HRBPs have line management responsibility for the HR Operations and shared service staff who support their allocated business unit, while HR generalists have no formal people management accountability within the HR function

HRBPs are more senior in grade and compensation but perform substantially the same function as HR generalists, with the title reflecting seniority and the scope of the business unit supported rather than a fundamentally different role design

questions.question: You are partnering a business unit that is planning a major reorganisation. The business leader shares the proposed structure with you confidentially before any announcement. You immediately recognise that the new structure creates a significant risk of discrimination claims based on the demographic profile of those at risk. What do you do?

options.text: Respect the confidentiality of the briefing and monitor the situation as the restructure progresses, intervening only if the demographic concern materialises into a specific complaint or legal challenge after the structure has been announced

Flag the risk immediately and directly with the business leader, quantify the demographic impact with data, recommend specific design changes before the structure is finalised, and document your advice as a formal record of HR's counsel

Brief your CHRO about the concern before raising it with the business leader, ensuring you have HR leadership backing before challenging a senior leader's restructure design in what may become a difficult and politically sensitive conversation

Raise the concern with Legal before taking it to the business leader, since a restructure that creates potential discrimination risk requires legal assessment of the exposure before HR frames its advice and makes any recommendations

questions.question: A business leader regularly contacts you at short notice for HR advice but rarely acts on your recommendations. Over time you notice they use HR as a sounding board rather than a decision partner. How do you shift the dynamic?

options.text: Accept the dynamic as valuable since being a trusted sounding board is a legitimate and important HR function, and pushing for more decision-making influence risks disrupting a relationship that is working well for the leader personally

Reduce your availability for short-notice calls to signal that your time and input are worth more than they are currently being treated and that a more structured engagement model would better serve both parties going forward

Have a direct conversation with the leader about the pattern you have observed, share your perspective on what a more effective partnership would look like, and agree a model that treats HR input as a genuine input to decisions rather than validation

Escalate to the CHRO to request a different business unit assignment since a leader who consistently disregards HR advice creates a context in which effective HRBP work is structurally impossible and personal time investment cannot be justified

questions.question: What is organisational design in the context of HRBP work, and what is the HRBP's role in it?

options.text: Redesigning the HR function's own operating model and service delivery structure to improve the efficiency and quality of HR services provided to the business unit in response to stakeholder feedback about the current HR experience

Shaping how a business unit is structured — spans and layers, role accountabilities, decision rights, and reporting lines — to optimise performance, advising the leader on design options and the organisational trade-offs of each

Managing the annual headcount planning and workforce forecasting processes for the business unit, ensuring that the people resource plan is aligned with the business unit's strategic and operational objectives for the coming year

Coordinating job evaluation and grading exercises to ensure internal pay equity and that role descriptions accurately reflect current accountabilities for the purposes of compensation benchmarking and regulatory reporting requirements

questions.question: You discover through data analysis that a particular manager's team has significantly higher attrition and lower engagement scores than peers. The manager is a top commercial performer and is well liked by senior leadership. How do you approach this?

options.text: Share the data with senior leadership and let them decide how to respond, since a manager who is well liked at senior level is best challenged by their own leadership chain rather than by HR raising the issue directly with the individual

Raise the data directly with the manager, frame it as a business risk rather than a personal criticism, work with them to understand root causes, and create a people leadership action plan with clear accountability and a defined review timeline

Monitor for a further quarter before taking action since attrition and engagement data can reflect external market factors rather than management behaviour, and intervening prematurely risks damaging a relationship with a high-value commercial performer

Recommend a formal team engagement survey to gather additional qualitative data before approaching the manager, since presenting engagement and attrition metrics alone may not give the manager sufficient specific and actionable feedback to act on

questions.question: A business leader tells their HRBP they want to restructure their team to remove a layer of management. What should the HRBP's first question be?

options.text: How many roles will be affected and what is the total redundancy cost including both statutory and contractual entitlements, so that a financial case can be constructed and approved before any further restructure planning proceeds

What business outcome is this restructure intended to achieve, and have alternative structural options been explored, so that the design decision is grounded in a clear purpose rather than an assumption about the right answer

Has Legal been consulted about the statutory consultation requirements and the redundancy selection process, since any restructure involving role eliminations creates legal obligations that must be factored in from the very outset

What is the desired timeline for completing the restructure, since the implementation timeline drives the sequencing of consultation, selection, and communication activities that HR will need to plan, resource, and support

questions.question: A business leader tells you they want to give their highest-performing team member a 25% salary increase, significantly above the compensation band, because they are worried about losing them. What is your role in this situation?

options.text: Support the increase without qualification since retaining top talent is a legitimate business priority and the leader who manages this person directly is best placed to assess their value and the genuine risk of losing them to a competitor

Refuse to support any out-of-band salary increase since compensation band exceptions undermine the integrity of the grading and pay framework and set a precedent that is difficult to manage equitably across the broader employee population

Explore the full retention picture, assess current market data for the role, advise on the equity implications for peers at similar performance levels, present multiple options including a structured exception process, and ensure a transparent approval decision

Recommend a non-monetary retention package such as additional leave, flexible working, or a development opportunity as an alternative to a pay exception that avoids the complications of taking someone permanently outside their compensation band

questions.question: A business unit is planning to hire 15 people in Q1 but the HRBP's workforce data shows the team already has capacity issues due to management overhead and unclear role accountabilities. The hiring plan has executive approval. What do you do?

options.text: Support the hiring plan as approved since it has executive sign-off and it is not the HRBP's role to challenge decisions that have been made at the right level of authority with full business awareness of the operational context

Raise the capacity and role clarity concerns with the business leader before hiring begins, present the supporting data, and recommend resolving the structural issues in parallel with or ahead of hiring to avoid embedding dysfunction at scale

Pause the hiring plan unilaterally and escalate the structural concerns to the CHRO, since adding 15 people to a team with existing capacity problems creates an organisational risk that overrides the executive-approved hiring decision

Flag the structural concern in the HRBP quarterly business review but allow the hiring to proceed in the interim, since the business has made a commercial decision and HR's role is to support rather than obstruct approved plans

questions.question: What is the 9-box grid used for in talent management, and what is its primary limitation?

options.text: A tool for setting individual performance targets and development objectives; its primary limitation is that it focuses on individual outcomes rather than team performance, creating a competitive dynamic that can undermine collaboration

A framework for assessing employees on performance and potential to identify talent tiers and succession candidates; its primary limitation is that potential ratings are inherently subjective and can systematically embed manager bias

A succession planning matrix designed specifically for senior leadership and director-level roles; its limitation is that it does not apply below director level and creates a two-tier talent approach that disadvantages junior high-potentials

A performance review calibration tool used to achieve a consistent rating distribution across teams; its primary limitation is that calibration sessions are time-consuming and tend to favour employees with more visible or vocal advocates

questions.question: You are partnering a leader who is highly directive, does not delegate, and consistently makes decisions without consulting their team. Engagement in the team is declining. The leader does not see their behaviour as a problem. How do you approach the coaching conversation?

options.text: Avoid a direct coaching conversation since unsolicited feedback on leadership style is outside the HRBP role and risks damaging a relationship that depends on the leader seeing HR as a business ally rather than as a performance assessor

Present the engagement data as a business risk, share specific behavioural observations with examples, connect the leadership style directly to the team performance impact, and invite the leader into a conversation about what they want the team to look like

Recommend a formal 360-degree feedback process before having any direct coaching conversation, since the leader is more likely to engage constructively with style feedback when it comes from multiple sources rather than solely from the HRBP

Escalate the leadership concern to the leader's line manager and let them address the behaviour directly, since a leader who does not see their style as problematic is unlikely to respond constructively to feedback delivered by their HR business partner

questions.question: You are asked by a business leader to recommend which of two equally-performing candidates should be promoted. You have limited direct observation of either candidate. What is your approach?

options.text: Make a recommendation based on the leader's stated preference since they have significantly more direct exposure to both candidates than HR does and their judgment about readiness should carry more weight than an HR view made from a distance

Recommend a structured assessment approach using a clear capability framework for the role, evidence gathered from multiple observers, and consistent criteria applied to both candidates rather than offering a personal recommendation from limited data

Recommend the more senior-tenured candidate as a default since length of time in role and accumulated institutional knowledge are reliable indicators of readiness that are unlikely to lead to a promotion decision that fails quickly

Recommend that both candidates be placed on structured development plans and the decision be deferred by six months until there is more evidence to differentiate between them and a stronger basis for making the choice

questions.question: Which of the following best describes strategic workforce planning in an HRBP context?

options.text: Planning and managing the annual headcount budget for the business unit in alignment with Finance, ensuring that recruitment spend is within approved parameters and that any variance is explained and approved through the finance governance process

Analysing the gap between the current workforce capability and the workforce needed to deliver the future business strategy, and designing interventions to close that gap through build, buy, borrow, or automate approaches

Producing monthly headcount and attrition reports for the business unit leadership team, providing the data visibility that enables leaders to make informed decisions about recruitment, retention, and team structure going forward

Managing the talent pipeline for critical roles identified in the business unit's succession plan, ensuring that each critical role has at least one identified successor who is actively being developed toward readiness for that position

questions.question: A business leader asks for your opinion on whether to dismiss a long-tenured employee who has been underperforming for over a year. The leader has done nothing formal about the performance issue until now. What is your honest assessment?

options.text: Support the dismissal since the leader is in the best position to assess whether the employee's performance has genuinely been inadequate and their judgment about the right outcome should take precedence over HR's procedural concerns

Be direct: the absence of any documented performance management process significantly weakens the employer's legal position, and proceeding to dismissal now without a proper prior process creates substantial unfair dismissal risk that must be addressed first

Recommend a settlement agreement as the most pragmatic route since it achieves the business outcome quickly while avoiding the reputational and operational risk of a formal capability process that will take time and management attention

Advise the leader to document three recent specific performance incidents as a retrospective foundation and then proceed to a disciplinary hearing, since documented examples provide sufficient legal cover for a performance-related dismissal decision

questions.question: You are the HRBP for a business unit whose leader is well-connected but is regularly described by their team as inconsistent, politically motivated, and difficult to trust. The leader has strong upward relationships. How do you navigate this?

options.text: Align with the leader's agenda and priorities since your effectiveness as an HRBP depends on maintaining a productive working relationship with the person you directly support, and challenging a well-connected leader creates more risk than it resolves

Build direct relationships with the team and bypass the leader where necessary, using your independent access to team members to provide support and gather intelligence without the leader's knowledge or explicit approval

Maintain a professional relationship with the leader, build independent credibility with the team, raise concerns with the leader directly when you observe specific problematic behaviour, and use people data to make organisational risk visible to senior leadership where warranted

Request a reassignment to a different business unit since the combination of a politically complex leader and a team with low trust creates a dynamic in which effective HRBP work is structurally impossible and personal credibility is at unacceptable risk

questions.question: What does it mean for an HRBP to be a credible activist in a professional HR context?

options.text: Being willing to challenge business decisions that breach employment law or expose the organisation to significant legal liability, ensuring that HR's primary function is to protect the company from legal and regulatory risk

Being trusted by the business and willing to take a clear position on people issues even when it is uncomfortable, combining deep business knowledge with a point of view on what is right and the courage to express it clearly

Advocating for employees in disputes with management, acting as an employee representative who ensures the employee's perspective is heard and fairly considered in all HR processes and significant business decisions

Actively promoting HR initiatives and programmes within the business unit through internal communications and stakeholder engagement, ensuring that people-focused projects receive the visibility and buy-in they need to succeed

questions.question: Your business unit is going through significant change and the leader asks you to help design a communications plan that frames all changes positively and does not mention any downsides. You know some changes will have a genuine negative impact on a portion of the team. What do you do?

options.text: Help design the communications plan as requested since controlling the narrative during change is a legitimate leadership prerogative and the leader is entitled to decide how to frame organisational changes to their own team

Refuse to help design any communications plan that is not immediately and fully transparent about every negative impact, since HR's credibility depends on never being associated with communications that employees will later perceive as misleading

Advise the leader that communications which minimise real impacts tend to damage trust more than the changes themselves, propose a plan that acknowledges impact honestly while communicating the support available, and explain the reputational risk of communications employees will see through

Prepare two alternative versions of the communications plan and present both to the leader, letting them choose which approach to take and ensuring HR has fulfilled its advisory role regardless of which option the leader ultimately selects

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of succession planning in an organisation?

options.text: To identify employees who are immediately promotion-ready so that vacancies at senior levels can be filled quickly from the internal talent pool without the cost and disruption of external recruitment processes for critical positions

To ensure the organisation has a pipeline of capable individuals who can fill critical roles when they become vacant, reducing dependency on external hiring and building long-term organisational resilience and continuity

To manage the planned transition of senior leaders who are approaching retirement, ensuring that knowledge transfer occurs systematically and in a structured way before the departure happens and institutional knowledge is lost

To document the individual development plans of high-potential employees, creating a visible record of the organisation's investment in its future leaders and demonstrating commitment to internal career progression

questions.question: A manager tells you they want to put a team member on a formal performance improvement plan (PIP). When you review the background, you find the manager has never given the employee any documented feedback about their performance concerns. What is your advice?

options.text: Support the PIP since formal performance processes are more impactful and motivating than informal feedback conversations and the employee is more likely to take their performance seriously when the situation is escalated to a formal level

Advise that moving directly to a PIP without prior documented feedback creates a legally weak process and is likely to feel procedurally unfair — recommend a structured documented informal conversation first before any formal step is taken

Draft the PIP but include an explanatory section acknowledging that prior feedback was not formally documented, framing the PIP as the starting point for a structured improvement process rather than the culmination of a documented prior process

Decline to support a formal PIP until the manager has conducted and documented at least three formal feedback conversations over a minimum three-month period, ensuring the process is procedurally robust before escalating to formal action

questions.question: A business leader asks you to justify the value of HR Business Partnering. They say they can get most HR support from the shared service centre and do not see what additional value you add. How do you respond?

options.text: Present your activity log showing the volume of HR interactions, advice provided, and issues resolved over the past quarter, demonstrating through the quantity of output that the HRBP role is actively used and therefore valuable to the business

Acknowledge the challenge directly, make a specific evidence-based case using examples of people decisions influenced, risks identified, and commercial outcomes contributed to, and offer to be more explicit going forward about the impact of your interventions

Escalate the challenge to the CHRO and ask them to engage with the leader to defend the HRBP model, since a senior leader questioning the value of the HRBP function is a structural issue that should be addressed at the HR leadership level

Offer to reduce your involvement and let the leader trial managing without dedicated HRBP support for a quarter, since the best way to demonstrate value is to allow the leader to experience what is absent when that support is removed

questions.question: An HRBP is preparing for a talent review for their business unit. Which data set is most important to include to make the conversation strategically useful?

options.text: A complete list of all employees in the business unit with their current performance ratings and grade levels, providing the talent review panel with a full population view from which to make talent tiering and development decisions

An integrated view of performance, potential assessments, flight risk indicators, critical role vacancy risk, and development readiness mapped against the business unit's strategic capability needs over the next 12 to 24 months

The top 10 percent of performers by grade level within the business unit, focusing the talent review conversation on the employees most likely to be identified as high-potential and succession candidates rather than reviewing the whole population

Succession candidates already nominated by the business unit leader in the prior cycle, updated to reflect any changes in performance or development status, providing continuity with previous talent review decisions and nominations

questions.question: You are supporting a restructure that will reduce the business unit from 45 to 30 people. The business leader wants to base selection for redundancy purely on performance ratings. You know the ratings process in this business unit has historically been inconsistent and influenced by personal relationships. What do you do?

options.text: Support the ratings-based selection since performance data is the most legally defensible selection criterion available and the historical inconsistency in ratings is a concern that should be addressed separately from the immediate restructure decision

Flag the inconsistency risk clearly, recommend a multi-criteria selection process validated by a calibration panel, and document your recommendation formally so the selection process can be defended if challenged legally by affected employees

Recommend selecting based on role criticality and business need rather than individual performance, since role-based selection avoids the legal and procedural complications of a ratings-based approach where the ratings have known reliability issues

Conduct a fresh independent performance assessment of all 45 employees before making any selections, commissioning an objective evaluation that is not influenced by the personal relationships that have distorted the historical ratings process

questions.question: What is the concept of psychological safety in a team context, and why is it relevant to HRBP work?

options.text: The absence of physical or psychological safety hazards in the work environment, which HR monitors through health and safety risk assessments and employee wellbeing surveys conducted as part of the annual people planning cycle

The shared belief among team members that they will not be penalised for speaking up, asking questions, or making mistakes — relevant because teams without it suppress the honesty and challenge that drive performance and surface organisational risk

An employee's individual right to take mental health-related leave without managerial scrutiny or any requirement to provide detailed clinical information as a condition of the leave being approved by HR or occupational health

A measure of an individual employee's emotional resilience and their personal capacity to manage workplace stress, assessed as part of the hiring and onboarding process to identify new joiners who may need additional wellbeing support

questions.question: You have been the HRBP for a business unit for 18 months and have built a strong relationship with the leader. You are now observing behaviour from the leader that is creating genuine employee relations risk, but you are concerned that raising it will damage the relationship. How do you approach this?

options.text: Prioritise the relationship by not raising the concern directly, instead monitoring the situation and intervening only if the behaviour escalates to the point where a formal HR process becomes unavoidable and the risk cannot be ignored

Raise the concern clearly and directly with specific examples and data, explain the business and legal risk in concrete terms, and accept that a trusted HRBP relationship must be capable of carrying challenge as well as support

Document your observations over a further two to three months to build a stronger evidence base before raising the concern, since a well-evidenced single conversation is more likely to land effectively than an earlier conversation the leader can dismiss as premature

Brief the CHRO about your concern before raising it with the leader, ensuring you have HR leadership support before initiating what may be a difficult conversation with a senior business leader who has strong organisational relationships

questions.question: A senior individual contributor tells you they are planning to resign because they have had no development conversation with their manager in over a year and feel invisible. The manager is unaware. What do you do?

options.text: Encourage the employee to raise their development concerns directly with their manager since the HRBP's role is to enable direct conversations rather than to mediate between employees and managers on matters within the manager's accountability

Acknowledge the concern, explore what would make the employee stay, facilitate an honest three-way conversation between the employee, manager, and yourself, and address the broader pattern with the manager if it reflects wider development neglect across the team

Raise a formal grievance on the employee's behalf for lack of career support, since the manager's failure to conduct development conversations over 12 months constitutes a breach of the employee's reasonable expectations of the employment relationship

Recommend that the employee explore internal transfer opportunities to a team with a more development-focused manager, since the working relationship with the current manager has deteriorated to a point where the development gap cannot realistically be closed

questions.question: What does span of control refer to in organisational design, and what are the risks of spans that are too wide or too narrow?

options.text: The geographic scope of a manager's accountability across multiple locations or countries; too wide creates travel and time zone burden that reduces management effectiveness, too narrow limits the cultural integration benefits of a global team

The number of direct reports a manager is responsible for; too wide reduces management attention and development quality for individuals, too narrow creates unnecessary hierarchy, slows decisions, and inflates management cost across the organisation

The range and depth of decision-making authority delegated to a manager within the governance framework; too wide creates inconsistency and risk across the organisation, too narrow slows down decisions and reduces manager accountability for outcomes

The number of distinct business units or functions an HRBP is expected to support simultaneously; too wide reduces depth of HRBP engagement with each business, too narrow makes the HRBP model operationally inefficient at scale

questions.question: You are supporting a new business unit leader who is technically excellent but struggling to build trust with their inherited team. Three months in, two key team members are considering leaving and the rest are disengaged. The leader is not aware of the severity. How do you approach this?

options.text: Wait for the situation to develop further since three months into a new leadership role is too early to intervene and doing so risks undermining the leader's authority before they have had adequate time to establish themselves with the team

Brief the leader's line manager about the team sentiment data and the specific flight risks and let the line manager address the situation directly, since a new leader hearing difficult feedback responds better when it comes from their own leader

Meet with the leader, share the team sentiment data and the flight risk information honestly, acknowledge the difficulty of inheriting a team, and co-create an accelerated trust-building plan while positioning yourself as a thinking partner rather than a judge

Commission a formal team engagement survey and share the results simultaneously with the leader and their line manager, since survey data provides a more objective and defensible basis for the conversation than qualitative intelligence from informal channels

questions.question: What does it mean to HR business partner at the strategic versus operational level, and why does the distinction matter?

options.text: Strategic HRBP work is performed with L1 and L2 leaders; operational work is performed with middle managers and individual contributors, and the distinction matters because different seniority levels require different relationship management approaches from HR

Strategic HRBP work focuses on future-state capability, organisational design, and people risk; operational work handles transactional HR issues — the distinction matters because HRBP time invested strategically generates substantially higher organisational value

Strategic HRBP work is performed by senior or principal HRBPs while operational work is the domain of junior HRBPs, and the distinction matters for team structure, grading, and defining the career progression pathway within the HRBP function

The distinction is largely semantic and varies by organisation, with most HRBPs in practice performing a blend of strategic and operational work that cannot meaningfully be separated into distinct categories with genuinely different value propositions

questions.question: You are leading a talent calibration session with four senior leaders. One leader is consistently rating their own team members higher than the evidence supports, while another is consistently rating theirs lower. How do you manage this?

options.text: Accept the ratings as submitted since leaders are accountable for their own talent assessments and challenging their judgments in a group forum risks creating conflict that makes the calibration less productive and more adversarial for everyone

Name the rating patterns directly in the session using calibration data, invite the group to apply a consistent evidence-based standard, and facilitate a structured challenge process where ratings must be supported by specific behavioural examples

Adjust the ratings yourself after the session to correct the distributional imbalance before finalising the talent output, since post-session normalisation is a standard calibration technique that ensures fair outcomes without creating unnecessary group confrontation

Hold separate bilateral conversations with each outlier leader after the session to discuss their rating patterns privately, then reconvene a smaller follow-up session to revisit the affected ratings without the pressure of the full group present

questions.question: A business leader wants to make a lateral move offer to a high-potential employee to develop them for a future VP role, but they have not told the employee about the VP aspiration and the lateral move appears to be a sideways step. How do you advise?

options.text: Support the move as planned since development intentions behind an offer are a business decision and disclosing the full VP aspiration creates formal expectations and obligations the business may not yet be ready to commit to in writing

Advise the leader that development moves work best when the employee understands the intent, recommend being transparent about the VP development trajectory so the employee can make an informed decision and stay motivated through the lateral step

Recommend against a lateral move entirely since high-potential employees should be progressed vertically rather than laterally and a sideways move that appears to be a step back risks triggering exactly the attrition risk the leader is trying to manage

Check whether the employee has a competing external offer before advising the leader on approach, since if an external offer exists the development framing needs to be stronger and the timeline for transparency needs to be accelerated significantly

questions.question: What is the primary risk of conducting a talent review process where managers complete potential ratings in isolation without calibration?

options.text: The process takes significantly longer than a calibrated review since managers must complete ratings independently before any group discussion occurs, reducing the overall efficiency of the talent management cycle for the year

Ratings reflect individual manager bias and inconsistent interpretation of potential criteria, producing a talent picture that cannot be meaningfully compared across the organisation and may systematically disadvantage certain demographic groups

Employees are more likely to be informed of their individual ratings when managers complete assessments independently, since the absence of a group calibration removes the social pressure that normally keeps rating conversations confidential

The HRIS system cannot accommodate un-calibrated individual ratings in its succession planning and talent visualisation modules, creating a data quality problem that requires manual workarounds and reduces the analytical value of the output

questions.question: The business unit you support has just been told it will not receive the headcount it requested for next year due to a cost reduction exercise. The leader is frustrated and is now considering making existing team members work across roles they were not hired for. What is your role?

options.text: Support the leader's decision to redistribute work across the existing team since managing within constrained resources is a line management responsibility and HR's role is to support business decisions rather than second-guess the leader's operational judgment

Acknowledge the resource pressure, help the leader prioritise what must be done versus what can be stopped or automated, assess whether expanding roles is contractually viable, and ensure the wellbeing and workload risk to the team is explicitly named and managed

Raise a formal challenge to the headcount decision by building a business case for the CHRO, since an under-resourced team creates people risks that HR has a responsibility to name and escalate to the appropriate decision-making level

Recommend outsourcing the additional work to a third-party provider to avoid the contractual and wellbeing complications of asking employees to work significantly outside the scope of what they were contracted and hired to do

questions.question: What does total rewards mean in a modern HR context?
options: The sum of base salary and annual bonus for an employee
The complete package of monetary and non-monetary value an employer provides, including base pay, variable pay, benefits, wellbeing, development, and the employee experience
The financial cost of the remuneration package per employee
The combination of salary and equity compensation offered to senior leaders

questions.question: A talent acquisition team is struggling to fill senior technical roles. Hiring managers say compensation offers are consistently being rejected. HR Ops data shows offer acceptance rates have fallen from 74% to 52% in six months. What is the most rigorous diagnostic approach?
options: Increase all offers by 15% and monitor acceptance rates
Conduct exit interviews with candidates who declined offers

Conduct a structured analysis of declined offer feedback, benchmark compensation bands against current market data for those specific roles, assess whether non-compensation factors are also driving declines, and present findings before making any pay changes
Recommend a signing bonus policy to make offers more competitive without adjusting base salary bands

questions.question: What is the difference between job evaluation and market benchmarking in compensation management?
options: Job evaluation assesses external pay competitiveness; market benchmarking determines internal equity
Job evaluation determines the internal relative value of roles based on responsibilities and impact; market benchmarking assesses how compensation compares to external pay rates for equivalent roles
Job evaluation is performed by HR; market benchmarking is performed by Finance
They are equivalent terms used interchangeably in different organisations

questions.question: During an annual compensation review, you identify that women in a particular job family are on average paid 11% less than men at the same grade with equivalent performance ratings and tenure. What do you do?
options: Flag the finding in the compensation report and recommend a review in the following cycle

Escalate immediately with a quantified remediation proposal, identify the root cause of the gap, prioritise corrections for the most severe cases in the current review cycle, and recommend a structural audit of the entire compensation framework to prevent recurrence
Seek Legal advice before communicating the finding to the CHRO
Adjust the data cut to control for additional variables before concluding there is a genuine gap

questions.question: What is a compa-ratio and what does it indicate?
options: A comparison of an employee's performance rating against their peer group average
A ratio comparing an employee's actual salary to the midpoint of their pay band, indicating their position within the band and relative pay competitiveness
The ratio of total compensation cost to revenue, used to assess workforce affordability
A compliance metric measuring the percentage of employees within grade

questions.question: A high-performing employee tells you they have a competing offer 30% above their current salary and will leave unless matched. The market data you have does not support a 30% increase for the role. How do you advise the business?
options: Match the offer — losing a high performer is more costly than the salary increase
Decline — matching counteroffers sets a dangerous precedent

Conduct a rapid market analysis for the specific role, assess the total cost of replacement against the retention investment, explore whether the full gap can be bridged through a combination of base, bonus, and non-monetary levers, and make a transparent recommendation to the business with a clear view of both the precedent risk and the replacement risk
Negotiate the employee down to a 15% increase as a compromise

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a performance calibration process in a pay-for-performance model?
options: To reduce the time managers spend writing performance reviews
To ensure that performance ratings are applied consistently across teams and that distribution reflects genuine performance differentiation rather than manager leniency or severity bias
To identify which employees should receive a performance improvement plan
To align individual performance ratings with business unit financial results

questions.question: An organisation is moving from an annual performance review to a continuous feedback model. Several senior managers are resisting, saying the new approach lacks rigour and makes compensation decisions harder to justify. How do you respond to this concern?
options: Revert to the annual review model — senior manager buy-in is essential for any performance process to work

Acknowledge the legitimate concern about compensation linkage, design a summary calibration mechanism that aggregates continuous feedback into a defensible rating for compensation purposes, and work with resistant managers to demonstrate how the new model captures more evidence, not less
Mandate the new approach and manage resistance through performance management of non-compliant managers
Run both models in parallel for a year before deciding which to retain

questions.question: What is a long-term incentive plan (LTIP) designed to achieve, and what is its primary retention risk?
options: To reward employees for long service; the retention risk is that some employees stay beyond their productive years solely to vest

To align senior employees' financial interests with long-term business performance by granting equity or cash instruments that vest over time; the retention risk is that employees leave once a tranche vests, creating cyclical attrition
To supplement pension contributions for senior employees; the risk is tax complexity
To compensate for below-market base salaries; the risk is that the LTIP does not vest if business targets are missed

questions.question: The finance team asks HR to cut the L&D budget by 40% to meet a cost reduction target. The L&D plan supports three business-critical capability gaps identified in the strategic workforce plan. How do you respond?
options: Agree to the cut — finance decisions take precedence over HR plans
Protect the full L&D budget — workforce capability is a strategic investment

Quantify the business risk of each capability gap, calculate the cost of the cut against the risk of the unmet capability needs, propose a prioritised reduced plan that protects the highest-risk investments, and present a joined-up view to the CFO and CHRO rather than accepting a blanket cut
Move the L&D spend to department budgets to take it off the HR cost line

questions.question: What is the primary limitation of using employee engagement survey scores as the sole measure of organisational health?
options: Engagement surveys are too expensive to run frequently enough to be useful

Survey scores measure sentiment at a point in time and can be influenced by recent events, do not capture performance or retention risk, and can mask important sub-group differences when only reported at an aggregate level
Employees rarely complete engagement surveys honestly when they know their manager will see the results
Engagement is not a reliable predictor of business performance

questions.question: A new CEO announces they want to move to a fully variable compensation model where all employees have a significant portion of pay at risk tied to company performance. HR is asked to design and implement this within six months. What are the critical risks you must surface before design begins?
options: The six-month timeline is too short — recommend 12 months

Surface legal risks around varying existing contractual terms, the behavioural impact of high pay-at-risk on psychological safety and risk-taking, the potential for the model to disadvantage lower-paid employees disproportionately, the need for transparent and credible performance metrics, and the likelihood of increased attrition during transition — all before design decisions are made
Conduct a market benchmarking exercise to validate the model against industry practice
Model the financial cost of the variable pay pool under different performance scenarios

questions.question: What is the difference between voluntary and involuntary attrition, and why does the distinction matter for talent management?
options: Voluntary attrition is managed exits; involuntary attrition is unmanaged. The distinction affects HR workload planning

Voluntary attrition reflects employees choosing to leave; involuntary is employer-initiated. The distinction matters because voluntary attrition — especially among high performers — signals something about the employee experience, culture, or leadership, while involuntary attrition reflects the organisation's performance management health
The distinction is primarily a payroll and benefits administration consideration
Voluntary attrition is measured quarterly; involuntary attrition is measured annually

questions.question: A manager rates an employee as exceptional (top 5%) but when asked to provide specific examples of exceptional performance, cannot give any beyond vague statements like they just get it. How do you handle this in a calibration session?
options: Accept the rating — managers know their teams and should be trusted

Challenge the rating constructively in the session, ask for specific behavioural evidence, and if none can be provided, apply the calibration group's standard that ratings must be evidenced — downgrading where the evidence does not support the top rating
Accept the rating but note the lack of evidence in the calibration record
Ask the employee to provide a self-assessment to supplement the manager's view

questions.question: What is the concept of pay transparency and what are the primary arguments for and against it in an organisational context?
options: Pay transparency means publishing all salary data publicly. It is argued to improve equity and reduce negotiation gaps but opposed due to privacy concerns and potential team conflict

Pay transparency refers to sharing pay band information with employees. Arguments for include reducing perceived inequity and improving trust; arguments against include risk of creating pay compression anxiety and increased administrative burden managing exceptions
Pay transparency is a legal requirement in all OECD countries. The debate is about implementation, not principle
Pay transparency is the same as pay equity — both refer to eliminating gender and race pay gaps

questions.question: Your organisation's onboarding satisfaction scores have declined for three consecutive quarters despite no change to the onboarding programme. New hire turnover in the first six months has increased. What is the most rigorous analytical approach to diagnosing the root cause?
options: Redesign the onboarding programme — the content has clearly become stale

Segment the data by hiring cohort, hiring manager, business unit, and role level to identify whether the decline is uniform or concentrated — then conduct structured new hire interviews to understand which specific onboarding elements are failing and whether external factors such as role clarity, manager quality, or team dynamics are the real drivers
Increase the length of the onboarding programme to provide more support
Survey existing employees about their onboarding experience retrospectively

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a salary band structure in compensation management?
options: To limit the maximum salary the organisation will pay for any given role

To create a framework that supports internal pay equity, ensures external competitiveness within a defined range, and provides a structure for pay progression that is transparent and consistently applied
To make annual pay review administration more efficient
To ensure compliance with national minimum wage legislation

questions.question: A business leader wants to recognise an entire team with a special bonus for delivering a high-stakes project under pressure. The bonus would be outside the normal cycle and above the approved budget. How do you advise?
options: Decline — out-of-cycle bonuses set a precedent and are outside policy
Support the bonus — recognising team effort is important for culture

Explore the recognition intent, advise on the right mechanism — whether a formal bonus, a non-monetary team recognition, or an accelerated in-cycle award — ensure proper approval authority is sought, and document the decision with clear criteria so it does not become an informal precedent
Recommend individual performance letters as a non-monetary alternative

questions.question: What does skills-based talent management mean, and how does it differ from role-based talent management?
options: Skills-based talent management focuses on technical skills; role-based focuses on leadership behaviours

Skills-based talent management organises workforce planning, hiring, development, and deployment around specific skill sets rather than fixed job roles, enabling more fluid allocation of talent to work — as opposed to role-based management which anchors people to static job descriptions
Skills-based talent management uses competency frameworks; role-based uses job families
They are equivalent approaches with different terminology used in different industries

questions.question: You are presenting talent review outcomes to the executive team. One executive asks why a candidate they personally sponsored for the high-potential list was not included. The candidate's data clearly shows they do not meet the criteria. How do you handle this?
options: Add the candidate to the list — executive sponsorship is a valid talent indicator
Agree to review the candidate again after the meeting

Acknowledge the executive's view directly, present the specific evidence against the criteria transparently, offer to discuss what development would close the gap and what timeline is realistic — holding the data-based decision while treating the executive's input with respect
Include the candidate in a separate emerging talent category to satisfy the executive without changing the high-potential criteria

questions.question: What does the term employee value proposition (EVP) describe?
options: The total financial cost of an employee to the organisation
The set of unique benefits, opportunities, and experiences an employer offers in exchange for an employee's skills, contributions, and commitment
The contractual terms offered to a new hire during the offer stage
The performance targets set for an employee during the annual review cycle

questions.question: Employee engagement scores in one business unit are 12 points below the company average, but the unit is hitting all its commercial targets. The business unit leader dismisses the scores, saying the team performs well and engagement surveys are not meaningful. How do you respond?
options: Agree with the leader — commercial performance is the ultimate measure of team health
Accept the dismissal and monitor the situation next year

Acknowledge the commercial performance but present the evidence that sustained low engagement is a leading indicator of higher attrition, knowledge loss, and performance risk — particularly for teams operating in high-skill domains — and recommend targeted action rather than a full engagement programme
Escalate to the CHRO to put the business unit under formal focus

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a total compensation statement provided to employees?
options: To meet regulatory disclosure requirements in jurisdictions with pay transparency legislation

To make the full value of the employment package visible to employees — including benefits, pension, insurance, and non-cash elements — improving perceived value of total remuneration and reducing attrition driven by incomplete salary comparisons
To provide a basis for salary negotiations during the annual review
To document the compensation package agreed at hire for legal purposes

questions.question: Your organisation uses a forced distribution performance rating model where exactly 10% of employees must be rated at the bottom tier. A manager tells you they genuinely do not have any bottom-tier performers on their team and refuses to apply the distribution. What is your advice?
options: Enforce the distribution — the model is company policy and must be applied consistently
Agree with the manager — forced distribution is widely discredited and should not be applied if it misrepresents performance

Acknowledge the tension, present the manager with the calibration evidence for their team relative to peers, advise on the company's official position, and — if the team's performance genuinely does not support bottom-tier ratings — escalate the concern about the model's application to HR leadership rather than asking the manager to falsify ratings
Apply the distribution by rating the lowest performer on the team at the bottom tier regardless of their absolute performance level

questions.question: What is the primary risk of setting overly aggressive short-term performance metrics in a variable pay plan?
options: Employees will feel the targets are unachievable and disengage from the plan
Short-term metric pressure can incentivise behaviours that optimise for immediate results at the expense of longer-term value, risk management, customer relationships, and team development
The plan will be too expensive to fund if all employees hit their targets
Performance metrics become difficult to calibrate across different business units

questions.question: Three of your top performers have recently been passed over for promotion and are now showing signs of disengagement. The business has a policy of minimum two years in role before promotion eligibility. How do you advise the business?
options: Enforce the policy — exceptions undermine consistency and fairness
Ignore the policy in this case — losing top performers is too costly

Assess whether the two-year rule is creating a retention risk that outweighs the consistency benefit, work with the business to explore whether the policy has a legitimate rationale or is an administrative default, and recommend a structured exception process for demonstrably promotion-ready talent rather than either ignoring or blindly applying the rule
Recommend retention bonuses as an alternative to promotion

questions.question: What does it mean to benchmark compensation at the 50th, 75th, or 90th percentile, and when would targeting each level be appropriate?
options: These percentiles represent pay grades within a salary band; the choice depends on employee performance

The percentile target indicates how the organisation's pay compares to the market distribution — 50th is median (pay to market), 75th is a premium strategy for talent attraction, 90th is reserved for highly competitive or scarce roles. The choice depends on the organisation's talent strategy, role criticality, and budget
Targeting the 90th percentile is always best practice to attract top talent
Percentile benchmarking only applies to base salary and is not relevant for total compensation

questions.question: You are asked to design a retention strategy for a GCC that has been experiencing 28% annual attrition in its first year of operation. Initial exit interview data cites better compensation elsewhere as the primary reason. What is your diagnostic and design approach?
options: Conduct a market compensation benchmarking study and adjust all salaries upward

Treat compensation as one potential cause rather than the confirmed cause — analyse whether attrition is concentrated in specific roles, managers, or cohorts, cross-reference exit data with engagement and tenure data, validate whether compensation is genuinely non-competitive or whether other factors are driving exits and compensation is a socially safe reason to cite, then design a targeted multi-lever retention strategy
Introduce a retention bonus scheme tied to 12-month service commitments
Benchmark against local competitors and match the top quartile for all roles

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an onboarding programme from a talent management perspective, beyond administrative induction?
options: To ensure new hires complete all mandatory compliance training within their first week

To accelerate time-to-productivity, build a sense of belonging and connection to the organisation's culture and purpose, and reduce early attrition by ensuring new hires have the relationships, clarity, and support to perform effectively from the start
To provide new hires with a comprehensive overview of all HR policies and procedures
To assess whether new hires are a cultural fit during their probationary period

questions.question: A senior leader is lobbying to exclude their team from a company-wide salary freeze, arguing their team's roles are market-scarce and they will lose people. Every other business unit is subject to the freeze. What is your position?
options: Support the exclusion — market scarcity is a valid business reason
Refuse — policy exceptions undermine the integrity of the freeze

Assess the market data for the specific roles claimed to be scarce, evaluate the attrition risk against the precedent risk of a visible exception, recommend a structured exception process through the right governance channel if the case is legitimate — and be consistent in applying the same standard to any other leader who requests an exception
Recommend a non-monetary retention package instead of a pay exception

questions.question: What is the difference between a merit increase and a cost of living adjustment (COLA)?
options: Merit increases are given annually; COLAs are given quarterly
A merit increase rewards individual performance and contribution; a COLA adjusts pay to maintain purchasing power in line with inflation and is not linked to individual performance
Merit increases apply to base salary; COLAs apply only to allowances and benefits
They are equivalent terms used differently in different countries

questions.question: Your organisation's learning and development function has historically focused on classroom training. You are asked to modernise the L&D strategy for a 5,000-person global workforce. What are the three most critical strategic shifts you would recommend?
options: Move all training online, introduce a learning management system, and reduce L&D headcount

Shift from scheduled event-based learning to embedded continuous learning; move from function-defined training catalogues to business-need-driven capability investments; and build manager capability as the primary vehicle for day-to-day development rather than relying on formal programmes
Invest in a new LMS platform, create a mentoring programme, and introduce mandatory learning hours for all employees
Partner with external universities to provide accredited programmes for all employees

questions.question: What is career pathing and why has its importance increased in the current talent market?
options: A tool for planning individual training calendars — important because employees want more structured development

A framework that makes visible the possible trajectories — vertical, lateral, and cross-functional — an employee might take within an organisation, and the capabilities required at each step; its importance has increased because employees in a tighter talent market increasingly choose organisations that offer visible growth and clear development stories over those that do not
A succession planning method for identifying high-potential employees — important because leadership pipelines are thinning
A compensation tool linking pay bands to career levels — important for pay equity compliance

questions.question: You receive exit interview feedback from five departing employees in the same team all citing their manager as the primary reason for leaving. The manager is not aware. The manager's line manager says the exits are coincidental. How do you proceed?
options: Accept the line manager's assessment — five departures may be coincidental

Treat a cluster of five manager-attributed exits as a significant signal, present the anonymised pattern to the business unit leader and the manager's line manager with the data, recommend a structured diagnostic conversation with the manager, and if the pattern is confirmed, design a specific intervention rather than a generic management development programme
Share the exit interview feedback directly with the manager
Wait for the next engagement survey to see if the pattern is confirmed before acting

questions.question: What is the concept of return on human capital investment (ROHCI) and what are the challenges of measuring it?
options: A ratio of HR department cost to total employee headcount; difficult to measure because HR cost allocation is inconsistent across organisations

A framework for quantifying the business value generated by people investments — including learning, development, and capability building — relative to their cost; the challenge is attributing business outcomes to specific people interventions when many other variables simultaneously affect performance
The relationship between employee tenure and productivity; difficult to measure because productivity definitions vary by role
A metric tracking the ratio of internal promotions to external hires; challenging because internal mobility data is often incomplete

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of employment at will in the United States?
options: An employee can resign at any time without notice, but the employer must provide 30 days notice before termination
Either the employer or the employee can terminate the employment relationship at any time for any reason, except for legally prohibited reasons such as discrimination or retaliation
Employment continues indefinitely unless a formal performance management process has been completed
Employers can only terminate employment at will during a probationary period

questions.question: Your company is expanding into a new country for the first time. The business wants to hire five employees in that market within 60 days. HR has no existing knowledge of local employment law. What is your approach?
options: Use the company's standard employment contract with a clause specifying the home country's law as governing law

Engage local employment counsel to advise on mandatory employment law requirements, determine the appropriate legal entity or employer of record structure, ensure contracts, benefits, and processes are compliant with local law before any offer is made, and build a go-live checklist that covers payroll, data protection, and statutory obligations
Mirror the employment terms used in the geographically closest country where the company already operates
Hire the employees as independent contractors to avoid the complexity of local employment law

questions.question: What is the primary obligation of employers under GDPR in relation to employee personal data?
options: To obtain explicit written consent from employees before processing any of their personal data

To process employee personal data lawfully, fairly, and transparently; collect only what is necessary for specified purposes; retain it no longer than necessary; and protect it with appropriate security measures
To store all employee data on servers located within the EU
To provide employees with an annual data audit report

questions.question: A manager wants to monitor employee productivity by installing software that captures screenshots of employees' screens every five minutes without their knowledge. The business operates in the EU. What is your advice?
options: Approve the monitoring — the company owns the equipment and has a legitimate interest in productivity

Advise that covert monitoring of this nature is likely unlawful under GDPR and potentially under national data protection law, recommend a transparent monitoring policy that is proportionate to the business need, requires employee notification, and is subject to a data protection impact assessment
Allow the monitoring but limit it to working hours only
Seek IT security clearance before responding to the manager

questions.question: What is the concept of joint employment and when is it most likely to create legal risk for a company?
options: When two companies share the cost of employing a single employee across both organisations

When a company exercises sufficient control over workers employed by a third party — such as a staffing agency or outsourced service provider — to be treated as a co-employer, creating exposure for employment law obligations including anti-discrimination, wage and hour, and collective bargaining
When employees in a joint venture are employed by both parent companies simultaneously
When a company acquires another and inherits its employment obligations

questions.question: An employee in your APAC region reports that their manager has been accessing their private messages on the company messaging platform and sharing them with other team members. How do you respond?
options: Advise the employee that company-owned platforms are subject to monitoring and the manager has not breached policy

Investigate whether the monitoring was within the bounds of any existing monitoring policy, whether the employee was notified of potential monitoring, and whether the sharing of messages with third parties was justified — treating the manager's conduct as a potential data protection and privacy breach pending the outcome
Escalate to IT to review the manager's access logs
Advise the employee to stop using the company messaging platform for private communications

questions.question: What triggers an obligation for collective information and consultation with employee representatives under EU law in a restructuring context?
options: Any restructuring that results in a change to an employee's reporting line

Collective redundancies meeting defined thresholds (typically 10 or more redundancies within a 30-90 day period depending on jurisdiction), transfer of undertakings, or other major changes to working conditions, triggering mandatory consultation with recognised employee representatives
Any restructuring affecting employees in more than one EU member state
A restructuring that results in a reduction in overall headcount of more than 5%

questions.question: The company is being acquired. As part of the due diligence process, the acquiring company requests a full data room of employee personal data including performance ratings, salaries, and HR records. How do you advise on the data sharing?
options: Provide the full data room as requested — M&A due diligence is a legitimate business process

Provide only aggregated, anonymised workforce data at due diligence stage, share identifiable personal data only when legally required and at the appropriate stage of the transaction under a data sharing agreement with appropriate confidentiality provisions, and conduct a data protection impact assessment before any personal data is transferred
Refuse to share any employee data until the acquisition is legally completed
Seek employee consent before sharing any personal data with the acquiring company

questions.question: What is TUPE (Transfer of Undertakings Protection of Employment) and what are the primary obligations it creates for the incoming employer?
options: A UK and EU framework requiring employers to consult unions before any redundancy

A framework that protects employees' terms and conditions when a business or service transfers to a new employer — the incoming employer inherits all existing employment contracts on their current terms, is prohibited from making TUPE-connected changes to terms, and must inform and consult employee representatives about the transfer
A framework requiring the outgoing employer to make employees redundant before a transfer to avoid inheriting liabilities
A voluntary best-practice standard for managing business acquisitions

questions.question: Your organisation operates a global HR system that stores employee data from employees in 40 countries on servers in the US. Several of those countries are in the EU. What compliance risk does this create and how should it be addressed?
options: No risk — US law applies to US-based servers regardless of where the data originates

GDPR restricts transfers of EU personal data to third countries without adequate protections — this requires either binding corporate rules, standard contractual clauses, or adequacy decisions covering the transfer, and the organisation should ensure compliant transfer mechanisms are in place and documented
The risk can be managed by anonymising all EU employee data before it is transferred to US servers
Notify EU employees that their data is stored in the US and obtain their consent

questions.question: What is the concept of indirect discrimination in an employment context, and how does it differ from direct discrimination?
options: Direct discrimination is intentional; indirect discrimination is unintentional. Both are unlawful
